title: Houthis Close Bab al-Mandab to Saudi Ships, Trump Slaps 50% Tariffs on Canada, Russia Kills 10 Aboard Black Sea Cargo Vessel
subhead: Yemen's Houthis declare a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia and shut the Red Sea's southern gateway to its vessels — a second chokepoint crisis alongside Hormuz. Meanwhile, Trump escalates trade war with 50% Canadian tariffs, and Russia's assault on Black Sea shipping turns lethal for civilian crews.

sections.heading: Houthis Declare Maritime Embargo on Saudi Arabia, Close Bab al-Mandab
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi movement announced an immediate 'maritime embargo' against Saudi Arabia on Monday, declaring the Bab al-Mandab Strait — the Red Sea's southern gateway — closed to Saudi vessels. Houthi military spokesman Yahya Sarea framed the move as retaliation for Saudi Arabia's blockade of ports and airports in Houthi-controlled northwestern Yemen, calling it 'an eye for an eye.' Houthi media official Nasruddin Amer wrote on X that the group's forces were 'closing Bab al-Mandab to Saudi enemy ships.'

The announcement marks the most significant escalation in the dormant Houthi-Saudi conflict since their 2022 truce, and it opens a second major maritime chokepoint alongside the Strait of Hormuz. Saudi Arabia has already diverted more than 70% of its crude exports — roughly 4 million barrels per day — through the Red Sea port of Yanbu to avoid Hormuz, according to Kpler and Signal Ocean data. The Houthis' move directly threatens that workaround. Saudi Arabia's foreign ministry condemned the announcement and vowed to 'take all necessary measures to protect its ships.' Mohammed Albasha of the Basha Report risk advisory told Reuters that even without attacks, the announcement alone would 'disrupt shipping and create uncertainty for Saudi ports.'
sections.whyItMatters: With Hormuz effectively closed and now Bab al-Mandab under threat, Saudi Arabia's two main oil export routes are both under pressure — a dual chokepoint scenario that could further tighten global energy supplies and drive prices higher.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran for 10th Consecutive Night as War Enters New Phase
sections.body:
The US military launched its tenth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, hitting what Central Command described as 'military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems.' Explosions were reported across Iran, from southern port cities including Jask and Sirik to the northwestern city of Tabriz — marking the first time the current campaign has reached that far north. The US also struck a civilian nuclear power plant under construction at Darkhovin, which Iran said had no military purpose.

Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes on Jordan, Kuwait, and Bahrain, with the IRGC claiming hits on hangars at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti airbase and Kuwait's Ali Al Salem airbase. Jordan's military said it shot down three Iranian missiles, while Kuwait and Bahrain also reported intercepted drones. Iran also said two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz had 'exploded' — claims disputed by CENTCOM. The UKMTO confirmed one tanker crew abandoned ship after being struck by a projectile.

Despite the fighting, diplomatic channels remain open. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei acknowledged receiving 'proposals' via mediators, while Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Washington 'always remains open to diplomatic solutions' but called Iran's strikes on US personnel 'an impediment.' Iran's Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf dismissed US claims of seeking peace, writing: 'Actions must confirm claims, not contradict them.'
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict is widening geographically — strikes on Tabriz and a nuclear facility mark escalation beyond the original southern coastal zone, while Iran's retaliation is drawing in more Gulf states. Yet both sides are keeping diplomatic backchannels open, suggesting neither wants the war to spiral into a full ground invasion.

sections.heading: Trump Imposes 50% Tariffs on Canada as Trade War Deepens
sections.body:
US President Donald Trump signed executive orders imposing 50% tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods, citing 'unequal treatment' of US cars, dairy, and alcohol. The duties, which take effect in 30 days, target everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks alongside industrial goods such as cement. Key exports including energy, potash, critical minerals, and fish are exempted. The tariffs apply regardless of whether goods fall under the existing USMCA free trade agreement.

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney responded by saying Canada was ready to 'intensify' trade talks, but accused the US of violating USMCA and cited 'threats to Canadian sovereignty' — a reference to Trump's repeated calls to make Canada the 51st state. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce called the tariffs 'regrettable' and urged meaningful progress in talks before they take effect. The Distilled Spirits Council of the US warned the move 'raises the risk of further retaliation.'

Notably, the executive orders make no mention of Canadian wildfire smoke — the pretext Trump had publicly floated. Instead, they list long-standing US trade irritants over cars, dairy, and alcohol, signalling a broader breakdown in negotiations. The tariffs were imposed under Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act, covering trade discrimination, rather than the emergency powers the Supreme Court struck down earlier this year.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the sharpest US-Canada trade escalation in decades. By applying tariffs that override USMCA, the US is effectively treating the trade agreement as dead — and Canada's measured response suggests it may seek to negotiate rather than retaliate symmetrically, but the window to avoid a North American trade shock is narrowing.

sections.heading: Russia Kills 10 Aboard Cargo Ship in Black Sea, Escalating War on Maritime Corridor
sections.body:
A Russian cruise missile strike on a Guinea-Bissau-flagged cargo ship off the Ukrainian port of Odesa killed 10 people, including four Indian nationals and one Ukrainian pilot, according to Ukraine's seaports authority and India's foreign ministry. The attack on the vessel Golden Leo is the deadliest single strike on a merchant ship since Russia's full-scale invasion began. Ukraine's regional head Oleh Kiper said 28 people have been killed in Russian strikes on the Odesa region in July alone.

The attack is part of an intensifying Russian campaign against Black Sea shipping. Foreign-flagged vessels use Ukraine's last remaining maritime export corridor along the Romanian and Bulgarian coastlines, carrying roughly two-thirds of Ukraine's $9 billion in annual agricultural exports. Russia appears to be deliberately targeting this route to deter foreign companies from trading through it. Ukraine's Prime Minister Serhiy Koretskyi said the government was 'preparing decisions that will enhance the safety of navigation and help protect civilian vessels and seafarers.'

On the offensive side, Ukraine claimed 183 Russian vessels had been struck in July alone, according to drone forces commander Robert Brovdi. Meanwhile, Russia's Belgorod region reported five people killed in a Ukrainian drone strike on a bus, and Moscow mayor Sergei Sobyanin said 400 drones were launched toward the capital overnight, though most were intercepted.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is deliberately making civilian maritime trade lethal — a tactic designed to strangle Ukraine's economy by making it too dangerous for foreign shipping companies to operate. The killing of Indian nationals also risks drawing new diplomatic pressure from a country that has tried to maintain ties with Moscow.

sections.heading: Hamas Elects Khalil al-Hayya as New Leader in Narrow Vote
sections.body:
Hamas elected Khalil al-Hayya as its new overall leader, defeating former political bureau chief Khaled Meshaal by a single vote — 35 to 34 out of 69 electoral college members. Al-Hayya, a 65-year-old Gaza-born operative who served as Hamas's chief negotiator during ceasefire talks, is seen as a continuity candidate from the Sinwar era. He survived an Israeli assassination attempt in Doha in September 2025 that killed five others, including one of his sons. Three more of his sons have been killed in previous Israeli strikes.

His election signals that Hamas's Gaza-based leadership — which bore the brunt of the war — has consolidated control over the movement, defeating Meshaal's more moderate exile faction that advocated moving away from armed confrontation. Al-Hayya's rapport with US envoy Steve Witkoff during October's ceasefire negotiations could provide a channel for future diplomacy, according to analysts at the European Council on Foreign Relations. However, al-Hayya has firmly rejected disarmament without guaranteed Palestinian self-determination, a position that puts him at odds with the US-led Board of Peace plan.
sections.whyItMatters: The single-vote margin reveals a deeply divided movement. Al-Hayya's win keeps Hamas on its current trajectory — resisting disarmament while maintaining diplomatic channels — but his slim mandate means any shift in strategy will face internal resistance from Meshaal's moderate faction.

sections.heading: India's 'Cockroach' Protesters March on Parliament as Police Crack Down
sections.body:
Tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) defied a police ban and attempted to march on India's parliament in New Delhi, demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and a broader crisis in India's education system. Police used tear gas and batons to disperse the crowds, with several protesters reporting injuries from cane charges. Mobile internet services were suspended in parts of central Delhi.

The movement takes its name from India's chief justice, who compared unemployed young people to 'cockroaches' during a court hearing — a remark that became a rallying point for widespread frustration over jobs, transparency, and accountability. The protests gathered fresh momentum after activist Sonam Wangchuk was forcibly moved to hospital on the 21st day of a hunger strike. Two CJP representatives met Health Minister JP Nadda, who said he would raise the matter within government, but the demonstration showed no signs of dissipating by evening.

The CJP has become one of the most visible expressions of public dissent against Prime Minister Narendra Modi in recent years, with students, parents, teachers, and professionals joining the cause. Inside parliament, opposition MPs repeatedly disrupted the opening day of the monsoon session over the same issues.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the largest youth-driven protest movement in India in years. The fact that it is drawing professionals and middle-class citizens alongside students suggests the grievances go well beyond exam leaks — pointing to a broader crisis of confidence in India's governance and opportunity structures.

bottomLine: The Houthis' closure of Bab al-Mandab to Saudi shipping opens a second maritime chokepoint crisis on top of Hormuz, directly threatening the workaround route Saudi Arabia has used to keep oil flowing. With the US-Iran war now in its tenth night of strikes, Trump's 50% tariffs on Canada deepening a North American trade rift, and Russia killing civilian sailors in the Black Sea, the global trade and security architecture is under simultaneous pressure on three fronts. Hamas's narrow leadership vote and India's swelling protest movement add political instability in regions already stretched thin.
